summary:
The siege and capture of Delhi (June–September 1857) was the central military episode of the [[event:2562|Indian Rebellion of 1857]], and the city's symbolic importance — as the seat of the last Mughal emperor, Bahadur Shah Zafar — made its recapture the essential British objective of the early campaign. The mutinous sepoys who had risen at Meerut in May 1857 marched to Delhi and proclaimed Bahadur Shah Zafar as their leader, transforming a military mutiny into a broader assertion of Indian sovereignty.

The British response was to establish a siege camp on the Delhi Ridge north of the city, holding an extraordinarily vulnerable position against repeated sorties and assaults from the city's large garrison. For nearly four months, a small and disease-ravaged British and loyal Indian force held the Ridge while reinforcements gathered, in one of the more remarkable defensive-offensive operations of colonial warfare. The arrival of the siege train and the 'movable column' under John Nicholson in August shifted the balance. The assault on Delhi began on 14 September 1857; Nicholson was mortally wounded leading the attack through the Kashmir Gate. After six days of brutal street fighting, the city fell to British forces on 20 September.

Bahadur Shah Zafar was captured in the Humayun's Tomb complex, where he had taken refuge, by Lieutenant William Hodson. His two sons and a grandson were summarily shot by Hodson on their return to Delhi. Bahadur Shah himself was tried and exiled to Rangoon, where he died in 1862, ending the Mughal dynasty. Delhi's recapture broke the rebellion's symbolic center; its fall, combined with the relief of Lucknow, effectively doomed the wider uprising.
